content.text: Executing Tools
Tool execution is the moment the agent's decision becomes a real action. It spans four steps: routing the
tool_use
block to the right function, validating arguments and permissions, running the implementation, and serialising the result into a
tool_result
block. Each step is a point where failures must be caught and surfaced cleanly.
Execution is where side effects happen.

Searching is reversible; deleting a file is not. Build your execution layer with the same care you would apply to any mutation that touches production data — validate before acting, handle errors explicitly, and make write operations idempotent where possible.
Synchronous Dispatch
The simplest execution pattern uses a
TOOL_REGISTRY
dict to map tool names to functions and calls them synchronously. This works well when tools are fast (< 1s) or when parallel execution is not needed:
Exception type
Likely cause
Handling
TypeError
Schema doesn't match function signature
Return is_error: True with arg error message
KeyError / IndexError
Missing field in result from external API
Catch and return is_error: True
ConnectionError
Network failure reaching external service
Retry or return is_error: True with hint
PermissionError
File or resource access denied
Return is_error: True with explanation
Any other Exception
Unexpected runtime error
Catch, log, return is_error: True
Async Concurrent Execution
When the model issues multiple
tool_use
blocks and the tools make network calls, running them concurrently with
asyncio
significantly reduces wall-clock latency. Each tool call becomes a coroutine executed in parallel:
Use
asyncio
when tools are I/O-bound (HTTP requests, database queries, file reads). For CPU-bound tools,
concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor
avoids the GIL. Both approaches produce the same
tool_result
list that feeds back to the model.
Permission Checking

Not every tool should be available to every caller. Attach a permission level to each tool and check the caller's permission before executing. Return a permission-denied error (not an exception) so the model can report it:
Gate destructive tools (delete, admin SQL) behind the strictest permission level
Return is_error: True for permission failures so the model explains the restriction to the user
Derive the user's permission from the authenticated session, not from the model's output
Never let the model request elevated permissions — resolve permissions server-side
Idempotent Write Execution

The model may occasionally generate the same tool call twice — after a retry, after an error that did not prevent the first execution, or due to a loop bug. Write tools should be protected against double-execution using an idempotency key:
The key is a hash of the tool name and input JSON. If the same call is seen within
IDEMPOTENCY_WINDOW

seconds, return the cached result without re-executing. This is a lightweight client-side guard — for critical operations (payments, order placement), use server-side idempotency keys in the underlying API.
Dry-Run Mode

During development and testing, you often want to verify the agent selects the right tools and generates correct arguments without triggering real side effects. A dry-run mode executes read tools normally and simulates write tools:
Set DRY_RUN = True in test environments
Read-only tools run normally in dry-run — their results feed real reasoning
Write tools return a simulated result indicating no changes were made
The model reasons over the simulated result as if it were real, revealing logic bugs without side effects
Execution Hooks

In production, you need audit trails, metrics, and rate-limit checks around every tool call. A hook pattern wraps the executor with pre- and post-execution callbacks without tangling that logic into individual tool implementations:
Pre-hooks run before execution: audit log, rate-limit check, permission re-verify
Post-hooks run after execution: metrics counters, notification triggers, result caching
Hooks receive a ToolExecutionContext with tool name, input, and turn number
Keep hooks lightweight — they run synchronously and add latency to every tool call
Call Budgets

An agent can get stuck in a loop calling the same tool repeatedly. Per-tool call budgets cap the maximum number of times a tool can be invoked within a single agent run, preventing runaway loops and accidental duplicate writes:
When a budget is exceeded, the
is_error: True

result tells the model it cannot call that tool again this run. The model should then either answer from what it has, ask the user for direction, or report that it cannot complete the task — rather than silently repeating the same call.
Sandboxed Code Execution

When a tool executes arbitrary code (a code-interpreter tool, a formula evaluator, or a user-supplied script), run it in an isolated subprocess with resource limits to prevent the model-generated code from accessing the network, reading sensitive files, or consuming unbounded resources:
Use subprocess.run with a timeout — never exec() model-generated code in the main process
Capture and truncate stdout/stderr before returning — outputs can be very large
Set resource limits (file descriptors, memory) via the resource module on Linux
For production code execution, use a full container sandbox (Docker, gVisor, Firecracker) — subprocess isolation is not sufficient against malicious code
Summary
Tool execution routes
tool_use
blocks through a
TOOL_REGISTRY
to implementations, validates permissions, runs the function, and serialises the result into a
tool_result
block with
is_error: True
on failure. I/O-bound tools run concurrently with
asyncio

to reduce latency when the model issues parallel calls. Write tools benefit from an idempotency cache to prevent double-execution. Dry-run mode lets you verify tool selection and argument generation in tests without real side effects.
